text.title: AI-Generated Test Audit Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grouping the 12 checklist items. Each item produces a pass/flag verdict; any flag blocks merge until repaired.

section.title: The five rules and their twelve items

rule.statement: For each test in the diff, classify the assertion class as behavior (state, output, side-effect, negative) or structural (was-called, no-exception, status-only). At least one assertion per test must be behavioral; structural assertions are allowed only as supporting checks, not as the sole assertion.

rationale.source: AI-generated-test failure pattern analysis; mutation-testing research on assertion strength

rule.rationale: AI assistants default to structural assertions because they are the safest to generate (do not require understanding business logic). A test with only structural assertions passes whenever the code compiles, regardless of whether it does the right thing.

example.title: Behavior assertion (passes)
example.code: def test_invoice_total_includes_tax(): invoice = create_invoice(items=[{"price": 100}], tax_rate=0.2) assert invoice.total == 120 # behavioral: actual value assert invoice.tax_amount == 20

example.title: Structural-only (flag)
example.code: def test_invoice_total_includes_tax(): invoice = create_invoice(items=[{"price": 100}], tax_rate=0.2) assert invoice.total is not None # structural assert hasattr(invoice, 'tax_amount') # structural

rule.statement: For every AC that has an error path or a documented failure mode, at least one negative-case test exists. Checklist items: (a) input validation failures, (b) external dependency failures (DB unavailable, API 5xx), (c) authorization failures (403/401), (d) state preconditions violated. AI-generated suites typically have zero of these; flagging is the default.

rationale.source: empirical analysis of GitHub Copilot test PR output; QA pattern research

rule.rationale: AI assistants strongly default to happy-path tests because failure-case generation requires reasoning about what could go wrong. A reviewer who flags missing failure cases recovers most of the test-suite value the AI did not provide.

rule.statement: For every numeric or bounded input, tests cover at least: zero, negative, exactly-at-boundary, one-over-boundary, large value (10x typical). For collections: empty, single-item, large. For strings: empty, very-long, unicode, whitespace-only. Checklist flags missing boundary cases per AC.

rationale.source: boundary-value testing canon; AI-test PR audits

rule.rationale: Boundary cases are where production bugs concentrate; AI test generators rarely produce them spontaneously. Naming them in the checklist makes the absence visible.

rule.statement: Mocks are limited to: (a) external services with documented unreliability, (b) time / random sources, (c) heavyweight components in unit tests. Forbidden: mocking the system-under-test itself, mocking the database when an in-memory or test-container alternative is cheap, mocking pure functions. Checklist counts mocks per test and flags tests with > 3 mocks for review.
rule.rationale: AI-generated tests default to mocking everything to avoid set-up complexity. The result is tests that pass whether the system works or not. The 3-mock heuristic catches the typical over-mocking pattern; explicit allowlist prevents collateral noise.

rule.statement: Test names describe behavior, not structure. Forbidden patterns: "test_function_X", "test_X_works", "test_X_returns_value". Required: "test_X_when_Y_then_Z". Tests run independently: no test depends on another's state; setup is per-test. Checklist items: name honesty (per test), independence (full-suite scan), unique fixtures (per test).
rule.rationale: AI generators produce generic names that obscure intent and assume shared setup that creates flaky inter-test dependencies. The checklist catches both.

section.title: The 12 checklist items in PR-review order

list.item: 1. Every test has at least one behavior assertion (not structural-only)
2. Every AC with error paths has at least one failure-case test
3. Numeric/bounded inputs cover zero / negative / boundary / over-boundary / large
5. Strings cover empty / long / unicode / whitespace where applicable
6. Mocks limited to external services, time/random, heavyweight components
7. No mock of the system-under-test itself
8. Test names follow when_X_then_Y pattern, not generic
9. Tests run independently (no shared mutable state)
10. Setup per-test (no test depends on another's effects)
11. AC mapping populated and walks behavior assertions
12. Mutation-test kill rate did not drop on changed files (when mutation-testing CI is on)
